pending_agent_instructions.microscopic: Low-cost baseline-first, no-new-calculation step. Do NOT run any new Amesp calculations. Use `parse_snapshot_outputs` (or the closest available parsing/listing operation) to enumerate candidate rotatable dihedral definitions from existing artifacts (preferably from the shared prepared structure and/or round_01 S0-optimized geometry). Provide a ranked list of at least 5 candidate dihedrals with atom indices and a brief label for each (e.g., aryl–vinyl, aryl–aryl, linkage to nsnc ring), and explicitly identify 1–2 dihedrals most adjacent to/within the nsnc-containing core. Also confirm which dihedral [56,0,1,2] corresponds to (bond type/location) so we can avoid it in the next torsion snapshot run. Reuse shared prepared structure context where helpful.

hypothesis_pool.name: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/aryl-alkene torsions) suppressing nonradiative decay in aggregates
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The molecule is a large, highly aromatic, multi-phenyl substituted conjugated system with ~10 rotatable bonds and many peripheral phenyl rings. Such architectures commonly have low emission in solution due to torsional/vibrational relaxation and become brighter upon aggregation when these motions are hindered. Shared context indicates high rotatable bond count (10) and many rings (10), consistent with rotor-driven nonradiative pathways being available in dilute solution and restricted in the condensed phase.

hypothesis_pool.name: Twisted intramolecular charge-transfer (TICT)-like excited-state twisting with aggregation suppressing access to a dark twisted state
hypothesis_pool.rationale: There is a heteroaromatic nsnc ring (N2S) embedded in an extended π system that could introduce polarity/CT character in S1 for some conformers. If S1 has partial CT and can twist to a dark state in solution, aggregation could impede that twist and increase radiative yield. However, donor/acceptor partition proxy is moderate and the structure is largely hydrocarbon, so strong CT/TICT is not assured.

hypothesis_pool.name: Excimer/exciplex-dominated aggregate emission (new emissive aggregate state rather than simple RIM brightening)
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The molecule is large and planarizable with extensive aromatic surface area, so π–π contacts in aggregates could form emissive excimers. Still, many bulky phenyl substituents can also frustrate close cofacial stacking, making excimer dominance less certain without packing evidence.

planner_diagnosis_history: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism for the provided molecule. Working leading hypothesis is RIM (restriction of intramolecular motion) because the structure is a large multi-aryl conjugated system with many peripheral phenyl rotors (rotatable_bond_count=10; aromatic_ring_count=10) that can enable efficient nonradiative decay in solution via torsional relaxation; aggregation is expected to hinder these motions and increase emission. This remains an initial hypothesis because we do not yet have direct excited-state evidence (e.g., whether S1 relaxes by twisting, whether oscillator strength collapses upon distortion, whether CT character emerges, or whether aggregate-state excimer emission is likely). Shared prepared structure context is available (ready; prepared XYZ/SDF with a selected conformer), enabling immediate macro-level structural analysis and low-cost microscopic S0/S1 probing. Specialized agents can: (Macro) analyze structural features, likely motions, qualitative packing tendencies, and map them to plausible AIE pathways; (Microscopic) run a bounded low-cost workflow (aTB S0 optimization and limited S1 vertical excitation/character indicators) but cannot reliably do exhaustive conformer/packing searches or heavy full-DFT excited-state relaxation for this 101-atom system in the first round. The microscopic baseline must stay low-cost and bounded to avoid expensive geometry optimizations and large-state sampling. First round should gather both macro and microscopic evidence to (i) identify the most plausible motional coordinates and (ii) see whether vertical S1 properties suggest LE vs partial CT and whether torsional distortion is likely to reduce radiative strength.

Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/aryl-alkene torsions) suppressing nonradiative decay in aggregates. Latest evidence modestly strengthens this hypothesis: macro descriptors show many rotors (10 rotatable bonds; 10 aryl rings; high branching) consistent with torsional IC pathways in solution, while microscopic baseline shows S1 is already bright at the S0 minimum (f~1.03), which weakens a TICT-dark-minimum scenario and is compatible with emission being mainly modulated by access to nonradiative torsional channels rather than an intrinsically dark S1. Key remaining uncertainty: whether twisting a key aryl–vinyl/aryl–aryl dihedral substantially reduces oscillator strength or changes S1 character (i.e., whether a dark twisted state is accessible). Best next step: run a minimal torsion-snapshot test (1–2 angles) to directly probe torsion sensitivity of S1 energy and brightness, the most discriminating low-cost observable still missing.

Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/aryl-alkene torsions) suppressing nonradiative decay in aggregates. Latest evidence leaves the hypothesis partially unresolved but overall modestly strengthens it versus TICT/excimer alternatives: macro proxies indicate many rotors (10) and high branching (18) consistent with strong intramolecular motion channels, while microscopic torsion snapshots show the lowest excited state remains strongly bright (f~1.03) even when twisting the chosen dihedral to 35° and 70°, with negligible changes in S1 energy (spread ~0.0016 eV) and oscillator strength (spread ~0.0002). Key remaining uncertainty: the torsion scanned (atoms 56-0-1-2) may not be the most conjugation-critical aryl–vinyl/central-core rotor; a different central dihedral could still enable a dark/twisted state (TICT-like) or more strongly modulate radiative strength. Best next step: perform a second, equally low-cost torsion snapshot on a more central conjugation-relevant rotor (preferably aryl–vinyl linkage near the heteroaryl-containing core) to ensure the negative torsion-sensitivity result is not an artifact of dihedral choice.

Leading hypothesis remains: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/aryl-alkene torsions) suppressing nonradiative decay in aggregates. Latest evidence leaves it partially unresolved: the torsion snapshots (+35°, +70°) show essentially no change in S1 energy (~3.0568→3.0552 eV) or oscillator strength (~1.0282→1.0280) and the lowest state remains bright, which weakens the specific idea that twisting along the probed dihedral opens a dark channel (TICT-like). However, because the tool repeated the same dihedral ([56,0,1,2]) rather than a more central conjugation-relevant rotor, the discriminating power is limited. Key remaining uncertainty: whether twisting a truly central aryl–vinyl/aryl–aryl bond adjacent to the nsnc-containing core can access a less-emissive (low-f) state or change state ordering/character. Best next step is a low-cost microscopic follow-up that explicitly forces a different dihedral selection and then parses per-snapshot outputs.

Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/aryl-alkene torsions) suppressing nonradiative decay in aggregates. Latest evidence leaves this partially unresolved and slightly weakens its discriminating support: repeated torsion-snapshot runs still probed the same dihedral [56,0,1,2] and found essentially no S1 energy/oscillator-strength sensitivity (ΔE~0.0016 eV; Δf~0.0002; lowest state remains bright). The key remaining uncertainty is whether a truly central conjugation-relevant rotor (especially adjacent to the nsnc-containing core) can access a darker/weakly allowed state upon twisting; current evidence cannot answer this because dihedral selection control failed. Best next action is a microscopic no-new-calculation parse step to enumerate/confirm available rotatable dihedrals from existing snapshot/structure artifacts, enabling a targeted follow-up on a different, core-adjacent dihedral.

macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the canonical SMILES and counts (rotatable bonds=10, aromatic rings=10, branch points=18), the structure is a highly substituted, multi-phenyl, aryl-alkene-conjugated scaffold with a small heteroaromatic unit (nsnc) embedded in an otherwise hydrocarbon-rich pi system. Dominant soft motions are expected to be phenyl-rotor twists about single bonds (biaryl-type linkages) and aryl–vinylene (C(sp2)=C(sp2)) torsions that modulate conjugation; these channels are classic nonradiative decay pathways in solution that can be suppressed upon aggregation (supporting RIM). A strong TICT scenario usually requires a clear donor–acceptor pair with substantial charge-transfer character and a rotatable donor/acceptor dihedral; here heteroatoms are few (3) and donor–acceptor partition proxy is mid (~0.5) rather than strongly segregated, making a pronounced D–A CT/TICT dark state less structurally compelled than torsional IC, though a weak ICT contribution via the heteroaromatic fragment cannot be excluded. Packing-wise, many peripheral phenyls and branching suggest steric congestion that disfavors close cofacial pi-stacking (reducing ACQ) and instead favors loose, restricted-rotation aggregates; however, extended pi surface and multiple aryls still leave some possibility of excimer-like interactions if partial overlap occurs.
Capability limit note: This response is limited to deterministic, low-cost structural/topology and proxy-geometry reasoning using the shared prepared context (no aggregate-state modeling, no explicit excited-state computations, no conformational ensemble simulation beyond given proxies). Any packing or TICT claims will be framed as plausibility based on structural motifs rather than confirmed energetics.

hypothesis_pool.name: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/aryl-alkene torsions) suppressing nonradiative decay in aggregates
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The molecule is a large, highly aromatic, multi-phenyl substituted conjugated system with ~10 rotatable bonds and many peripheral phenyl rings. Such architectures commonly have low emission in solution due to torsional/vibrational relaxation and become brighter upon aggregation when these motions are hindered. Shared context indicates high rotatable bond count (10) and many rings (10), consistent with rotor-driven nonradiative pathways being available in dilute solution and restricted in the condensed phase.

hypothesis_pool.name: Twisted intramolecular charge-transfer (TICT)-like excited-state twisting with aggregation suppressing access to a dark twisted state
hypothesis_pool.rationale: There is a heteroaromatic nsnc ring (N2S) embedded in an extended π system that could introduce polarity/CT character in S1 for some conformers. If S1 has partial CT and can twist to a dark state in solution, aggregation could impede that twist and increase radiative yield. However, donor/acceptor partition proxy is moderate and the structure is largely hydrocarbon, so strong CT/TICT is not assured.

hypothesis_pool.name: Excimer/exciplex-dominated aggregate emission (new emissive aggregate state rather than simple RIM brightening)
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The molecule is large and planarizable with extensive aromatic surface area, so π–π contacts in aggregates could form emissive excimers. Still, many bulky phenyl substituents can also frustrate close cofacial stacking, making excimer dominance less certain without packing evidence.
